title: Firm-Style Probability Drills
subtitle: Jane Street–style puzzles: symmetry, updating, and quick EV
intro: Trading firms like Jane Street, SIG, and Optiver do not ask you to recite formulas — they watch you reason under uncertainty. The patterns repeat: compute an expected value, exploit symmetry, update beliefs when new information arrives, and explain your answer out loud.

This capstone lesson collects original drills inspired by those interview styles (probability & markets guides, classic puzzle collections, and public practice sites). Nothing here is copied from proprietary question banks; the scenarios are written for Probability Playground.

Treat each question like a live interview: state assumptions, show arithmetic, and sanity-check extremes. If you can pass this lesson, you are ready to pair our interactive content with the external resources hub for depth reading.

questions.question: Two fair coins are hidden. You are told at least one is Heads. What is the probability both are Heads?

explanations.A: 1/4 is P(both Heads) unconditionally, but we are conditioning on at least one Head.
explanations.B: Correct. Conditional sample space {HH, HT, TH} — only one of three equally likely outcomes has two Heads.
explanations.C: 1/2 ignores that HT and TH are also consistent with 'at least one Head'.
explanations.D: 2/3 would require two favorable outcomes out of three for double Heads, which is not the case.

remediation.question: A fair coin is flipped twice. Given the first flip is Heads, P(both Heads)?

explanations.B: Correct — given first H, second is H with probability 1/2.
explanations.C: 2/3 is the 'at least one H' conditioning, not 'first is H'.
explanations.D: First flip fixed as H does not force second H.

remediation.question: Two dice show at least one six. Approximate P(both sixes)?

explanations.B: Correct — 11 outcomes have ≥1 six; only (6,6) has both.
explanations.C: 1/6 treats the second die as independent without conditioning.
explanations.D: Too high — double six is rare even conditional on ≥1 six.

questions.question: You roll a fair die; you win $6 if it shows 6, otherwise you lose $1. What is the expected profit per roll?

explanations.A: Underestimates the win frequency — one-sixth of rolls pay +6.
explanations.B: $0 is wrong — EV = (1/6)(6) + (5/6)(−1) = 1 − 5/6 = 1/6 ≈ $0.17, a small positive edge.
explanations.D: $1 EV would require much more favorable payouts.

remediation.question: Win $16 on a six, lose $2 otherwise. EV per roll?

explanations.A: The win on a six (1/6 of the time) more than offsets the five losses of $2.
explanations.C: Slightly overstates it — the exact EV is $1.
explanations.D: Too high for this payoff structure.

remediation.question: Win $10 on heads, lose $4 on tails (fair coin). EV?

explanations.A: Ignores the +10 win half the time.

questions.question: Fair die roll. Fair value is 3.5. You quote 3 at 4 with a $1 spread. A trader sells to you at 3. Did you likely buy above or below fair value?
options: Below — you got a good buy

explanations.A: Buying at 3 when fair is 3.5 means you paid less than fair — a good buy for you.
explanations.B: Correct. Your bid at 3 is below expected value 3.5, so buying there is +EV for the market maker.
explanations.C: 3 ≠ 3.5; you traded on the bid side below midpoint.
explanations.D: Fair value comparison is enough to judge the trade direction.

remediation.question: Fair value 10. You sell at your ask of 11. Relative to fair, you:

explanations.A: Selling above fair is good for the market maker.
explanations.B: Correct — ask above fair value is +EV sale.
explanations.D: This is about price vs fair, not inventory alone.

remediation.question: Fair value 50. Quote 49 at 51. Midpoint of your quote is:

questions.question: Urn: 2 red, 1 blue. Draw one — red. You draw again without replacement. P(second is red)?

explanations.A: 1/3 is P(first red), not second after first red.
explanations.B: Correct. After one red removed, urn has 1 red, 1 blue → P(red) = 1/2.
explanations.C: 2/3 was P(first red); second draw has fewer reds left.
explanations.D: Second draw is not certain red.

remediation.question: 3 red, 2 blue. First draw red. P(second red)?

explanations.A: Need to update counts: 2 red, 2 blue remain.
explanations.C: 3/5 is too high after removing a red.

remediation.question: Two cards from a deck without replacement. First is an ace. P(second is ace)?

questions.question: A and B each independently pick 0 or 1 uniformly at random (no communication or shared strategy). You see the sum mod 2. If it is 0, the team wins $3. Entry fee is $1. Should the team play?

explanations.A: Even with independent random picks the sum is even half the time (both 0 or both 1), so the team wins with prob 1/2 — positive EV.
explanations.B: Correct. Win $3 with prob 1/2, pay $1 → EV = 1.5 − 1 = 0.5.
explanations.C: Overstates EV — the team wins only half the time.

remediation.question: Same game but win $5 with prob 1/2, fee $1. EV?

explanations.C: Would need higher win or higher win prob.

remediation.question: Win $3 with prob 1/3, fee $1. EV?

questions.question: You are very uncertain about fair value on a novelty dice game. Should your bid-ask spread be wider or narrower than when you are confident?

explanations.A: Correct. More uncertainty → wider spread to protect against adverse selection.
explanations.B: Tight spreads when uncertain invite getting picked off on both sides.
explanations.C: Good market makers adjust width to confidence.
explanations.D: Both sides typically widen symmetrically around fair value.

remediation.question: New public info sharply reduces your uncertainty. Spread should:

explanations.A: Less uncertainty usually allows tighter quotes.
explanations.B: Correct — you can compete more aggressively when risk is lower.
explanations.D: You still need a spread for inventory risk.

remediation.question: Main reason market makers widen spreads:

explanations.B: Correct — compensation for risk and adverse selection.

questions.question: Repeated fair coin flips until first Heads. Expected number of flips?

explanations.A: You often need more than one flip.
explanations.C: Correct — expected waiting time for success prob 1/2 is 2 trials.

remediation.question: Flips until six on a fair die. Expected rolls?
Success prob 1/3 each trial. Expected trials until success?

questions.question: From a standard deck, two cards drawn without replacement. P(both hearts)?

explanations.D: 1/4 is P(one card is heart), not both.

remediation.question: P(both aces from two cards)?
One heart drawn. P(second is heart)?

questions.question: In a quant interview, which habit helps most when stuck?
options: Stay silent until you have the exact answer
Talk through assumptions and try small cases
Ask to use a calculator immediately
Change the problem to an easier one without telling the interviewer

explanations.A: Silence hides your reasoning — interviewers grade the process.
explanations.B: Correct. Narrating assumptions and testing small cases is exactly what firms like Jane Street encourage.
explanations.C: Mental math matters; calculators are rarely the first move.
explanations.D: Never silently change the problem — clarify with the interviewer instead.

remediation.question: Best first step on an unfamiliar puzzle:
Interviewer gives a hint. You should:

explanations.C: They want your reasoning, not theirs read aloud.

questions.question: Order-of-magnitude: about how many fair coin flips give an expected count of 50 Heads?

explanations.C: Correct — expected heads = n/2, so n = 100 for 50 heads.

remediation.question: Fair coin: expected Heads in 80 flips?
Quick sanity: 1000 flips, expected Heads near:

placementQuestions.question: Expected value of fair die roll?
At least one head in two fair flips — how many outcomes in sample space?

explanations.B: That counts favorable, not total space.

placementQuestions.question: Geometric with p=1/2, mean trials until success?
